overview: This image appears to show a Central American bushmaster (Lachesis stenophrys), a large venomous pit viper native to Central America. Bushmasters are known as the longest venomous snakes in the Western Hemisphere, with some individuals reaching lengths of over 10 feet. They are characterized by a pronounced middorsal ridge, especially on the posterior quarter of their body. These snakes have potent venom and are considered among the most dangerous vipers in their habitat. iNaturalist +4 5 sites Central American Bushmaster (Lachesis stenophrys) · iNaturalist

This image appears to show a Central American bushmaster (Lachesis stenophrys), a large venomous pit viper native to Central America.
Bushmasters are known as the longest venomous snakes in the Western Hemisphere, with some individuals reaching lengths of over 10 feet.
They are characterized by a pronounced middorsal ridge, especially on the posterior quarter of their body.
These snakes have potent venom and are considered among the most dangerous vipers in their habitat.
